cataloging_subject.label: Lord Grey de Ruthyn to Griffith ap David ap Griffith
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Reginald Grey's Middle English reply to Gruffudd ap Dafydd ap Gruffudd, written after Gruffudd's letter of 11 June and no later than Grey's report of 23 June 1400. It is not the whole three-letter dossier, Cotton MS Cleopatra F III as a physical manuscript, Hingeston's volume or editorial notes as a whole, the University of Michigan encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. Hingeston's paratext and his excerpts from the two related letters remain separately scoped content parts.

catalog_summary.title: Lord Grey de Ruthyn to Griffith ap David ap Griffith
catalog_summary.author: Reginald Grey, Lord Grey de Ruthyn
catalog_summary.editor: Francis Charles Hingeston; supporting excerpts transcribed by Henry Ellis
catalog_summary.date: After 11 June and no later than 23 June 1400
catalog_summary.region: The Welsh Marches, especially the Ruthin lordship, Chirkland, and Oswestry; exact place of composition unstated

identifiers.value: Cotton MS Cleopatra F III, f.70b

titles.value: Lord Grey de Ruthyn to Griffith ap David ap Griffith
notes: This is an editorial heading rather than a manuscript title or authorial superscription.

titles.value: Reginald Grey to Gruffudd ap Dafydd ap Gruffudd

assertion.notes: A modernized access title using normalized name forms, not a second historical title.

titles.value: Gruffuth ap David ap Gruffuth. We send the greting welle, but no thyng with goode hert.

agents.name: Reginald Grey, Lord Grey de Ruthyn
Gruffudd ap Dafydd ap Gruffudd
notes: The inspected sources do not supply a stable modern authority identifier.
Authorship concerns the historical letter; the surviving f.70b item is described by Hingeston as a contemporary copy attached to Grey's report to the Prince of Wales, not as an autograph.

scope.description: The complete printed item and its represented editorial layers
Passages that Hingeston explicitly reprints from Ellis's 1827 edition

date_statements.display: After 11 June and no later than 23 June 1400
notes: Gruffudd's initiating letter is dated 11 June. Grey's 23 June report says that he had sent an answer and enclosed its copy; Hingeston therefore dates the answer on or before 23 June. The exact day and place written are not stated in the represented text.

date_statements.display: Contemporary copy extant with Grey's report of 23 June 1400

places.label: Welsh Marches
notes: The conflict described concerns Grey's lordship and Gruffudd's activity along the north-eastern Welsh Marches.
This is historical setting and association, not a claim that the letter was composed at a particular Marcher place.
Grey was Lord Grey of Ruthin and his 23 June enclosing report was written at Ruthin; neither fact proves that the reply itself was composed there.

language_statements.label: Middle English

scope.description: The historical letter and both embedded historical-letter excerpts

notes: Modern English editorial headings and notes are paratext, not a second language facet for the abstract medieval work.

form_statements.description: A prose letter whose closing threat forms an intentional seven-line rhyming doggerel passage.

scope.description: The address, recapitulation, rebuttal, and most of the threat

form_statements.description: Most of Grey's reply is epistolary prose.

scope.description: Seven-line rhyming threat beginning 'But we hoope we shalle do the a pryve thyng'

form_statements.description: Norman Davis identifies Grey's seven lines as an intentional example of occasional rhyming doggerel.

subject_statements.label: Lordship conflict in the Welsh Marches
Prelude and context to the Glyndŵr rising

assertion.notes: Historical context supplied by Hingeston and the current manuscript catalog; Owain Glyndŵr is not named in Grey's reply.

summaries.value: Reginald Grey answers Gruffudd ap Dafydd ap Gruffudd's complaints point by point, rejects responsibility for John Wele's alleged betrayal, rebuts threats against Grey's men and lordship, invokes loyalty to the king and his law, and threatens Gruffudd with hanging for receiving stolen horses. The closing threat shifts from prose into seven lines of intentional rhyming doggerel.

CME00124 encodes Hingeston's Letter XVI on printed pp.35-38, including the main reply, its heading, marginal summary, witness note, textual notes, extensive excerpts from Gruffudd's 11 June letter reprinted from Ellis, and the final editorial reconstruction with an excerpt from Grey's 23 June report to the Prince of Wales. The initiating and enclosing letters are supporting related works, not parts of Grey's reply.

Hingeston describes the target as a contemporary paper manuscript pasted on the back of Grey's letter to the Prince at f.70b. The current British Library codex inventory describes f.70 itself as parchment. These claims are compatible if the paper letter-copy is an attached document mounted on the verso of a parchment host leaf; carrier and host support must not be conflated.

extent.description: Four modeled content parts contain 8 direct main-letter paragraphs, 1 heading plus 7 note containers of editorial paratext, 9 quoted paragraphs from Gruffudd's letter, and 1 quoted passage from Grey's report to the Prince, for 26 navigational items. These units overlap at the representation level where a NOTE container frames quoted text, so they are not a count of independent historical works. The XML has 1 DIV1, 1 HEAD, 23 P (8 direct and 15 inside notes), 7 NOTE, 5 PB, 3 HI, and 4 SUP elements. Davis identifies the closing threat as seven lines of rhyming doggerel even though the XML serializes it inside one P rather than L elements.

content_parts.label: Grey's reply to Gruffudd
content_parts.summary: The complete represented reply: address, point-by-point recapitulation, rebuttal, accusation concerning two stolen horses, royal-law argument, and closing threat of hanging.

locators.value: DIV1 direct child P elements (8), excluding embedded NOTE content
printed pp.35-38; Internet Archive PDF pages 155-158

content_parts.label: Hingeston's heading and editorial paratext
content_parts.summary: The item heading, source and support statement, marginal synopsis, identifications, textual note, quotation framing, and reconstruction of the three-letter sequence.

locators.value: pp.35-38, plus contextual preface discussion at pp.xxii-xxiii

notes: Some NOTE containers also embed the separately modeled historical-letter excerpts; item counts therefore document XML/editorial containers, not mutually exclusive prose units.
The printed closing 'Written, etc.' is Hingeston's editorial abbreviation and is not cataloged as a historical explicit or title.

content_parts.label: Excerpts from Gruffudd's letter of 11 June 1400
content_parts.summary: Selected passages from Gruffudd's initiating complaint, quoted in two footnotes so readers can compare Grey's recapitulation with the complained-of betrayal, the stolen horses, mutual threats, and the dated subscription.

content_parts.summary: The sentence in Grey's 23 June 1400 report stating that he sent Gruffudd's letter to the Prince together with a copy of the answer he had sent back.

related_works.title: Gruffudd ap Dafydd ap Gruffudd to Reginald Grey, 11 June 1400
notes: The initiating letter to which CME00124 responds; Ellis prints it as Letter II from Cotton MS Cleopatra F III, f.72.

related_works.title: Reginald Grey to the Prince of Wales, 23 June 1400
notes: The enclosing report with which Grey sent Gruffudd's letter and a copy of his answer; Ellis prints it as Letter I from Cotton MS Cleopatra F III, f.70.

evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD, IDG, letter DIV1, heading, eight direct paragraphs, seven notes, and five page breaks
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for the identifiers, encoded title, main reply, editorial notes, two supporting-letter excerpt groups, language declaration, page markers, and represented ending.

Confirmed 10,747 bytes; 1 DIV1, 1 HEAD, 23 P of which 8 are direct letter paragraphs and 15 are inside notes, 7 NOTE, 5 PB, 3 HI, and 4 SUP elements. The two Gruffudd excerpt notes contain 10 P including one framing paragraph; the final contextual note contains 5 P and one Grey-to-Prince quotation.

evidence.citation: Francis Charles Hingeston, ed., Royal and Historical Letters during the Reign of Henry the Fourth, vol. 1 (London: Longman, Green, Longman, and Roberts, 1860), Letter XVI, 35-38.
evidence.locator: context at printed pp.xxii-xxiii; target Letter XVI at printed pp.35-38 = PDF pages 155-158 = Internet Archive leaves n154-n157
evidence.summary: Immediate print-source evidence for the title, addressee and author, contemporary paper-copy statement, f.70b locus, complete reply, selected Ellis excerpts, reconstructed three-letter sequence, 11-23 June date interval, and attachment of the answer-copy to Grey's report.

evidence.citation: Henry Ellis, Original Letters, Illustrative of English History, Second Series, vol. 1 (London: Harding and Lepard, 1827), Letters I-II, 1-7.
evidence.locator: Letter I printed pp.1-4 = PDF pages 20-23; Letter II printed pp.5-7 = PDF pages 24-26
evidence.summary: Direct edition evidence for Grey's report from f.70 and Gruffudd's initiating letter from f.72. Comparison confirms that Hingeston reprints selected passages rather than either complete Ellis letter.

evidence.citation: British Library, Cotton MS Cleopatra F III, Archives and Manuscripts catalogue record 040-001103831.
evidence.locator: title, date, languages, physical characteristics, origin, digitised-content field, and access statement
evidence.summary: Official current evidence for shelfmark, record and ARK identifiers, Acts of the Privy Council context, 1375-1424 codex date, England-and-Wales origin, 213-folio extent, fire damage, mixed parchment/paper inventory, f.70 parchment host leaf, f.72 paper context, and current physical-request route. The record exposes no digitised-content URL.

evidence.citation: Norman Davis, 'Style and Stereotype in Early English Letters,' Leeds Studies in English, new series 1 (1967): 7-17.
evidence.summary: Scholarly evidence that Grey's seven closing lines are intentionally rhymed and belong to a broader late-medieval practice of occasional rhyming doggerel.
notes: The locally cached PDF is readable, but qpdf reports inconsistent linearization hint-table/shared-object entries and exits 3 after completing its check.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00124 item page.
evidence.summary: Official item route and stable UMDL identifier for the keyed electronic text.
notes: The canonical URLs remain recorded, but the automated final request encountered an access challenge; the exact derivative XML is retained in the pinned repository submodule.

open_questions.question: Where exactly was Grey's reply composed?
next_steps: Inspect the complete f.70b attached paper document and any endorsement, address, or lost-text evidence.
Do not infer Ruthin merely from the dateline of the separate 23 June enclosing report.

open_questions.question: What are the exact physical construction, attachment method, dimensions, and present foliation of the paper answer-copy mounted at f.70b?
next_steps: Request color images of all of f.70 recto and verso, transmitted-light or attachment detail if the British Library permits, and adjacent surfaces.
Keep the paper document and parchment host leaf as distinct physical layers until directly inspected.

open_questions.question: Is there a complete public digital facsimile of Cotton MS Cleopatra F III outside the current British Library catalog interface?
next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services whether unpublished or legacy photography exists.
If ordering, request the whole physical object and map the target separately to f.70b, with related letters at f.70 and f.72.

open_questions.question: Does the CME repository's software-worded root Unlicense intentionally apply to XML markup and data in CME00124?
next_steps: Request a path-specific clarification from the repository maintainer.
Until clarified, do not broaden the root software wording into a confirmed public-domain grant for markup or data.

notes: TEXT/@LANG='enm' describes the historical text layers. Modern editorial English in Hingeston's heading and notes is deliberately excluded from work-level viewer facets.
CME00124's seven-line doggerel is serialized inside a prose P; the mixed-form classification rests on Davis's explicit scholarly identification, not on XML L tagging.
Hingeston's quoted supporting passages are mediated through Ellis. Their common manuscript shelfmark does not justify bypassing Ellis in the modeled passage lineage.
The XML proofing date 8-31-16 is repository encoding history, not a work, witness, edition, or publication date.
